content.en:
Hyper-Parity

A zero-algorithm method for solving corners in Domino Reduction

Rodney Kinney, June 2020

Motivation

In the course of learning techniques for the fewest-moves challenge (FMC) event, I was often frustrated trying to find a finish to a Domino Reduction (DR) start. I would build blocks only to end up with bad parity. I would attempt a half-turn reduction (HTR) and discover that the corners were off. The most consistent advice I heard was to look for a DR with “easy” corners, but even this was not obvious to me. Ortega and VOP algorithms were usually too long or required lengthy setups. I decided to categorize all of the DR corner cases and ended up with a method that gives the shortest solution to solve corners from any DR configuration. This document describes that method.

When are corners in HTR?

When the cube is in HTR, it can be solved using only half turns. Bringing all F/B stickers to the F/B faces is necessary for HTR, but not sufficient. Let’s call the distribution of stickers on the F/B faces the FB orientation. This cube configuration has the correct FB orientation, but is not in HTR:

A cube with correct UD and FB orientation, but incorrect parity.

This configuration has two diagonal corners swapped, as in a Y-perm, so it has odd parity. Is fixing parity enough to bring the cube into HTR? No. Below is a configuration that has correct FB orientation and even parity, but is still not in HTR. It has incorrect UD orientation, needing a a UBL-UFR-DFL corner commutator to solve.

A cube with correct parity and FB orientation, but incorrect UD orientation.

To be in HTR, three conditions must be satisfied: parity correct, UD orientation correct, and FB orientation correct. Half turns do not change any of these three conditions, so our strategy will be to count half turns as neutral and track quarter turns as we bring the cube into HTR. Since we are in Domino Reduction, the only quarter turns available are U/U’/D/D’.

Parity

Parity is normally defined as even or odd, by the number of pairwise swaps that must be executed to solve the cube. We will think of it as the number of quarter turns needed to bring the cube into HTR, either zero or one. There are two parity states, P0 and P1, and each quarter turn changes the cube from one state to the other.

P0

P1

UD Orientation

There are many possible U/D sticker configurations, but since we are counting half-turns as neutral we will consider any configurations that are separated by only half-turns as the same UD state. We can think of UD orientation in the same way as parity, i.e. the number of quarter turns needed to bring the cube into HTR. This is the motivation for the name “hyper-parity.” There are three possible UD states.

The UD0 state,

all possible U/D sticker configurations that can be reached

from a solved cube using only half turns.

The UD1 state,

all U/D configurations that can be reached

from a solved cube using exactly one quarter turn.

The UD2 state,

all U/D configurations that can be reached

from a solved cube using exactly two (non-consecutive) quarter turns.

How do quarter turns affect the UD state? Any U/U’/D/D’ move will change the state in the same way, depending on which stickers are showing on the U face. It is possible to stay in the UD0 or UD2 state after a quarter turn, but not the UD1 state. The figure below shows how the U face determines the change in the UD state.

UD0

UD1

UD2

Possible transitions of the UD state following a quarter turn,

depending on the configuration of the U face.

FB Orientation

Just as before, we treat all configurations that are separated by only half-turns as the same state, and label states by the number of quarter turns needed to bring the cube into HTR. There are four FB states. To recognize them, we treat F/B stickers as equivalent (shown as green) and R/L stickers as equivalent (shown as red).

The FB0 state

  • All F/B stickers on the F/B faces

The FB1 state

  • Any F/B/R/L configuration that can be reached from FB0 using exactly one quarter turn
  • Four mis-oriented corners that can be brought to the U face using half turns

The FB2 state

  • Any F/B/R/L configuration that can be reached from FB0 using exactly two (non-consecutive) quarter turns
  • Four mis-oriented corners that cannot be brought to the U face using half turns

The FB3 state

  • Any F/B/R/L configuration that can be reached from FB0 using exactly three (non-consecutive) quarter turns
  • Two or six mis-oriented corners

Like before, any U/U’/D/D’ quarter turn will change the FB state in the same way, depending on which stickers are showing on the U face. A quarter turn can leave the FB1 and FB3 states unchanged, but not the FB0 and FB2 states. The figure below shows how the U face determines the change in the FB state.

FB0

FB1

FB2

FB3

Possible transitions of the FB state following a quarter turn,

depending on the configuration of the U face.

Visualizing Corner Solutions

Although it may seem like the number of DR corner configurations is large, we have reduced them to just 24 cases: 2 parity states X 3 UD states X 4 FB states. We can visualize the state of the cube corners by plotting points on a 3x4 grid, with the UD state on one axis and the FB state on the other. Half turns do not change our location on the grid, and every corner turn moves us to an adjacent location (and swaps parity). Let’s visualize some familiar algorithms (starting from a solved cube, i.e. the upper left corner).

FB0

FB1

FB3

FB2

UD0

UD1

UD2

Basic trigger:

R2 U R2 U’

Also describes:

R2 U F2 D’

Or:

R2 D’ R2 F2 U

Or:

F2 U’ F2 U2 B2 U’

etc.

FB0

FB1

FB3

FB2

UD0

UD1

UD2

Basic trigger:

U R2 U’

Also:

U’ F2 U’

Or:

U’ F2 R2 D

Or:

U R2 D2 L2 U’

etc.

Each transition from one point of the grid to another corresponds to a U/U’/D/D’ quarter turn. In between, any number of half turns moves may be inserted. The half turns change the U face configuration, and therefore affect where the next transition will end up, but given a particular U face configuration, all quarter turns result in the same transition. The variants in these diagrams all affect the corners in the same way, but affect the edges differently.

FB0

FB1

FB3

FB2

UD0

UD1

UD2

R2 U2 F2 U R2 U’

Or:

F2 D2 R2 U’ F2 U’

etc.

Parity is determined by whether the number of transitions is even or odd. Returning to UD0/FB0 will not solve the corners if done in an odd number of transitions.

Finding Corner Solutions

Finally, we have a procedure for solving the corners from an arbitrary DR configuration. Analyze the position to determine UD orientation, FB orientation, and parity. Find the corresponding point in the grid, and plot a path to UD0/FB0 with an even number of transitions if parity is even, or an odd number of transitions if parity is odd. Unfortunately, not all transitions on the grid are possible. For example, horizontal transitions are only possible on the UD0/UD2 rows, and vertical transitions are possible only on the FB1/FB3 columns. The way in which the UD and FB states combine add further restrictions. For example, the UD2/FB0 state is not achievable without disassembling the cube. Some states, shown below, can be combined in multiple configurations that are not reachable from one another using only half moves. In such cases, the different configurations will allow different sets of transitions.

UD0/FB1

A

B

A - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R are all the same color

B - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners are split 2/2

UD1/FB1

A

B

A - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners are in diagonal orientation

B - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners are in bars orientation

UD1/FB2

A

B

A - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners don’t match other stickers on same face

B - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners are in diagonal orientation

C - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners are all the same color

D - U/D stickers on mis-oriented F/R corners match one other sticker on same face

C

D

UD2/FB3

A

B

A - U/D stickers on the mis-oriented F/R corners match 2 other stickers on same face

B - U/D stickers on the mis-oriented F/R corners match no other stickers on same face

Configurations of combined UD/FB states that are not reachable from each other using only half turns. All UD/FB combinations not shown have only one configuration.

Below is the “Hyper-Parity Maze”, showing all possible transitions between DR corner states.

Hyper-Parity in FMC

The “Hyper-Parity Maze” gives a direct move count estimate to solve any corner configuration. Each transition on the grid represents one quarter turn plus 1-3 half-turns. A rule of thumb is that “sharp” direction changes on the grid (90 degrees or more) require 3 half-turn setup moves, while “gentle” direction changes require only one setup move. Parity affects the move count, because it determines whether the total number of transitions needs to be odd or even. The actual optimal solution will take the most direct route to the upper left corner, although there may be multiple routes with the same move count.

FR0

FR1

FR3

FR2

UD0

UD1

UD2

0

11

5

7/12

10

7/12

4/12

10

10

4

10

Approximate move count to solve corners from different states, when parity is even

FR0

FR1

FR3

FR2

UD0

UD1

UD2

12

10

10

2/7

12

2/4

10

6

8

10

6

Approximate move count to solve corners from different states, when parity is odd

A standard technique in FMC is to find a DR with “good” corners, solve the corners along with as many edges as possible, then use insertions to solve the remaining edges. Using hyper-parity, there really are no “hard” corner cases, only “long” corner cases, so any DR can be checked relatively quickly. Even corner cases with relatively high move count (e.g. more than 8 moves to solve) can yield a good skeleton. This is because each time we make a transition on the grid, there are several alternative set up moves available, each giving an opportunity to build blocks or otherwise permute edges. Here is an example scramble to illustrate:

Scramble: U' R2 L2 F2 B2 R2 F2 B2 U' B2 R2 L2 U' B2 R2 F2 U2 R2 L2 F2 U2 B2 L2 U F2 B2

Solution:

D B2 R2 U2 B2 R2 B2 D’ R2 D U2 L2 D’ B2 (14)

Explanation:

This scramble has only one pair and is not what most would consider an easy corner case. Nevertheless, a 14-move finish is pretty good. To find the solution, we note that the cube starts in the UD2/FB3-B state, with even parity. The most efficient corner solution takes 9 moves:

FB0

FB1

FB3

FB2

UD0

UD1

UD2

U’ // UD2/FB3-A

D2 B2 U // UD2/FB2 + 2x2x2

L2 D’ // UD1/FB1

L2 D’ // UD0/FB0

B2 // 2e2e3e (9)

For each transition, we have simply chosen the setup move that yields the most U/D blocks. The solution is easily adjusted to solve the E-layer, leaving a 2e2e insertion.

D // UD2/FB3-A

R2 (*) D’ // UD2/FB2

U2 R2 D // UD1/FB1

U2 L2 D’ // UD0/FB0

B2 // 2e2e (10)

(*) - R2 B2 R2 U2 B2 R2 B2 U2 (8-4)

Discussion

Getting the cube to a Domino Reduction state is relatively well understood, and explained in the excellent guide by Alexandros Fokianos and Tommaso Raposio. However, I’ve certainly seen many newcomers (myself included) struggle with the finish after reaching DR. No doubt it’s better in some cases to solve edges and leave a corner commutator, and no doubt many cubers have internalized how to solve the most useful DR corner cases intuitively anyway. I don’t know whether, in an FMC event, it’s worth the time to check all “bad” corner cases using this method, and I don’t know whether it’s worth the effort to internalize the Hyper-Parity Maze (as I have done) in order to do so. Nevertheless, I do know that I have never seen a written guide that covers how to find minimum-move solutions for any DR corner case, and I do know that it was helpful to me to put this guide together. I also know that I hope others find it useful.
